# Transcrição: 02-Second Person

- **Fonte:** 02-Second Person
- **Duração:** 03:04
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

I didn't have you practice the first person because that's the way you've been telling stories all your life. There was no need to practice, but this is new and practice is important. Here's what I want you to do. Take a story that you have told. It can be your own story, but ideally you probably have a story in your life that you tell about someone other than yourself. It could be a story you tell about your parents or your grandparents, a buddy that did a crazy thing once, a story from college, a story from the workplace, something that happened to someone other than you and ideally you are not present for it. But it's a great story, the kind of story you love to tell. Take a minute, find one. I know you have it. A story about someone other than yourself, an old family story. And I want you to tell it again, but this time I want you to tell it in the second person. I want you to tell it in the second person by making choices carefully about what you want your audience to look at. I focused my story on hands and mittens and boots and ice, the things that I thought were important, the things that I thought expressed the love and the concern and the care that this couple shared. I want you to choose carefully too. I want you to tell that story in the second person to someone. Go tell it to someone, but focus on the things that you think are most relevant to the story. Imagine a camera. Imagine pointing the camera at the things that you think are important and describing only through that camera. The beauty of the camera, both in first person and second person storytelling, is the limitations of the camera, the things that they don't show us. It's almost through the limitations of the camera that we better tell our stories, rather than telling a story through a wide camera that allows everyone to see everything. It's through the narrow lens of that camera that we're able to focus our audience's attention on only the things that we find important. So do that now. Pick the story. Figure out what's important in the story. Tell that story in the second person, thinking about telling it through the lens of a camera. Practice it often. Get good at it. Once you learn how to tell stories and you become adept at telling stories from the second person, you can start making surprising emotional connections and provoking emotional responses from audiences. You probably became emotional a little bit, no matter how black your soul might be. You probably became a little bit emotional about that story I just told you. Even though you don't know those people, you will never meet them in your lives. They mean nothing to you. You probably became a little emotional while I was telling it, because I used the second person, because I told you what to look at, because I focused your attention on the things

## [03:00 - 03:04]

that matter. You have to learn to do the same thing. Start with your first story now.
